title: ECG — Performing
oneLiner: Recording a diagnostic 12-lead ECG; essential for detecting arrhythmia, ischaemia, and conduction disease; poor technique causes misdiagnosis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **STEMI:** Patient with chest pain, new left bundle branch block (LBBB) or ST elevation ≥1mm in limb leads / ≥2mm in chest leads. This is the waveform that mandates immediate senior review, aspirin, and catheter lab activation. Do not finish the ECG if you see evolving ST elevation – show the tracing to a senior immediately.
- **Malignant arrhythmia on the tracing:** While performing, the screen shows ventricular tachycardia, torsades de pointes, or extreme bradycardia (<30 bpm). If the patient is symptomatic (syncope, hypotension), call for help — don’t remove leads; start CPR if no pulse.
sections.Presentation:
- **Indications for performing (typical scenarios):**  
  - Chest pain, dyspnoea, palpitations, syncope, pre-operative assessment, altered mental status, trauma (especially high-velocity).
- **Key exam findings affecting technique:**  
  - Patient’s chest shape (e.g., pectus excavatum, breast tissue, obesity) → may require alternative electrode positions.  
  - Hairy chest → requires shaving for electrode contact.  
  - Tremor (e.g., Parkinson’s, anxiety) → may cause muscle artifact – reposition arms, ask patient to relax.  
  - Inability to lie flat (e.g., severe COPD, heart failure) → record semi-upright; note on tracing.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
(Not a differential diagnosis list – instead, differentiate between true abnormality vs. artifact during acquisition; this primes pattern recognition on the tracing.)
- **True ST elevation** (MI, pericarditis, early repolarisation) vs. **artifact** (e.g., muscle tremor, wandering baseline due to poor skin contact, lead swap – e.g., misplaced V3 giving a false Q wave).
- **Sinus arrhythmia** (normal) vs. **atrial fibrillation** (irregularly irregular) – check lead II rhythm strip length and patient symptoms.
- **LBBB** vs. **paced rhythm** – look for pacer spikes before each QRS; if no spikes, it’s LBBB.
sections.Investigations:
The ECG itself *is* the investigation. This section covers how to perform it correctly.

- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Confirm patient identity, indication, and that they have given verbal consent (if conscious).  
  - Position patient supine (or semi-recumbent if unable). Arms relaxed, legs uncrossed.  
  - Clean skin – alcohol wipe or dry gauze; shave hair if necessary. Remove any oily film or moisturiser.  
  - Apply limb electrodes to fleshy parts of forearms and calves – **not** over bone or major tendon.  
  - Apply precordial electrodes:  
    - V1: 4th intercostal space (ICS), right sternal edge.  
    - V2: 4th ICS, left sternal edge.  
    - V3: midway between V2 and V4.  
    - V4: 5th ICS, midclavicular line.  
    - V5: 5th ICS, anterior axillary line (or horizontally from V4).  
    - V6: 5th ICS, midaxillary line.  
  - **Crucial:** In women, place V4–V6 *under* the breast tissue (by lifting the breast), not on top of it.  
  - Ensure all electrodes are firmly attached – poor contact causes wandering baseline.  
  - Press the “Auto” record button – machine will record 10 seconds of each lead.  
  - Observe the screen during recording:  
    - If artifact appears (e.g., baseline wander, 50 Hz interference), check leads and ask patient to breathe normally or hold breath briefly.  
    - If muscle tremor, ensure patient’s arms and legs are relaxed – may need to place arm electrodes higher on shoulders.  
- **Machine settings (verify before pressing record):**  
  - Paper speed 25 mm/sec (standard).  
  - Calibration 10 mm/mV (standard).  
  - Filter settings: use “diagnostic” mode (0.05-100 Hz) – avoid “monitor” filter (0.5-40 Hz) which can distort ST segment.  
- **When to repeat or add leads:**  
  - If patient has inferior ST elevation (II, III, aVF) → record right-sided ECG (V4R placed at 5th ICS right midclavicular line) to detect right ventricular involvement.  
  - If patient has posterior suspicion (e.g., anterior ST depression, tall R in V1) → record posterior leads V7 (left posterior axillary line at 5th ICS) and V8 (left mid-scapular line).  
- **Bloods (if ECG abnormal):** troponin, U&E (for hyperkalaemia), glucose (for hypokalaemia) – but these are not part of the performance itself.  
- **Imaging:** Not required as part of ECG performance.  
- **Special tests:** For suspected arrhythmias intermittent, consider ambulatory monitoring (Holter) or event loop recorder – not relevant at point-of-care 12-lead.  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** The **Modified Sgarbossa criteria** for diagnosing STEMI in the presence of LBBB:  
  - Concordant ST elevation ≥1mm in leads with positive QRS.  
  - Concordant ST depression ≥1mm in V1–V3.  
  - Discordant ST elevation ≥5mm.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Right-sided ECG** in any patient with inferior ST elevation – omission can miss right ventricular infarction, which changes fluid management (avoid nitrates, fill with IV fluids).
sections.Management:
(Management of the procedure, not of the underlying condition.)

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Perform ECG as above.  
  - If patient is unstable (chest pain, hypotension, shock), prioritise treatment over perfect technique – a 4-lead rhythm strip may be faster; however, a 12-lead should follow ASAP.  
  - If you observe ST elevation or new LBBB, show the tracing to a senior doctor immediately (do not wait for machine interpretation).  
  - Label the ECG clearly: patient name, date, time, indication.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - For patients with abnormal ECG, ensure the tracing is reviewed by a clinician within 15 minutes (locally defined).  
  - Repeat ECG for any new symptoms or after interventions (e.g., thrombolysis, PCI).  
  - If poor quality (artifact, missing leads), re-record – do not accept suboptimal traces.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Not applicable to performing the initial ECG.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - No specific orders for the procedure itself. If ECG shows ACS, follow local chest pain pathway (e.g., aspirin 300mg PO, GTN 400mcg SL, monitoring).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
No drugs are required for performing an ECG. However, if during the procedure you identify STEMI or tachyarrhythmia needing immediate treatment:  
- **First-line agents for suspected ACS (per NICE):**  
  - Aspirin 300 mg orally, stat (no contraindication).  
  - Glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) 400–800 mcg sublingually, repeated if needed (avoid if systolic BP <90 mmHg or suspected right ventricular infarction).  
- **For symptomatic bradycardia:** Atropine 500 mcg IV bolus, repeated up to 3 mg (per UK Resuscitation Council).  
- **Key contraindications:** GTN is contraindicated in patients who have taken sildenafil/vardenafil in past 24 hours (or tadalafil in past 48 hours). Atropine – avoid in haemodynamically unstable non-sinus rhythms (e.g., third-degree AV block – use transcutaneous pacing instead).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for acute doses.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving GTN sublingually before checking for right ventricular infarction (inferior STEMI). This can cause profound hypotension. Always consider right-sided ECG before giving GTN.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Patient vasovagal syncope (due to anxiety, lying flat, or pain from electrode removal) – remove electrodes, lie supine, raise legs.  
  - Skin irritation or allergic reaction to electrode gel – use hypoallergenic electrodes if known allergy.  
  - Missed ischaemia due to artifact – re-record if any doubt about quality.  
- **Chronic:**  
  - None from the procedure itself.
sections.Prognosis: The ECG is a diagnostic tool – prognosis depends on the underlying condition. A normal ECG in a chest pain patient does not rule out MI (especially NSTEMI); proceed with troponin.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Shave hairy chests before applying electrodes** – it reduces artifact and improves ST-segment fidelity. Use a disposable razor; avoid alcohol wipes on abraded skin.  
- **Document any lead placement deviation** directly on the ECG (e.g., "V4 placed under right breast," "patient sitting up due to dyspnoea") – this prevents false interpretations.  
- **Perform a right-sided ECG in any inferior STEMI** – it changes management (avoid nitrates, give fluids). Don't rely on standard 12-lead.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Placing limb electrodes on bony prominences or over major tendons** – causes muscle tremor artifact. Use fleshy parts of forearms and calves.  
- **Placing V4-V6 on top of breast tissue in women** – leads to small QRS voltages and falsely poor R-wave progression. Lift the breast to place electrodes in correct intercostal spaces.  
- **Accepting a poor-quality tracing** – baseline wander, 50 Hz interference, or missing leads can mask ST elevation or arrhythmias. Always re-record if the trace is not clean.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New chest pain, palpitations, or syncope.  
  - ECG result discussed with patient? If abnormal but deemed non-urgent, explain findings simply and mention that symptoms require re-attendance.  
  - If ECG was normal but patient had symptoms, instruct to re-present if symptoms recur.  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - If abnormal (e.g., left ventricular hypertrophy, old MI), arrange GP follow-up or cardiology outpatient, depending on local policy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**  
  - ECG shows ST elevation, new LBBB, or clear signs of acute MI.  
  - Rhythm strip shows VT, torsades, asystole, or extreme bradycardia with symptoms.  
- **Call crash team if:** patient becomes unconscious, pulseless, or has a respiratory arrest while leads are attached.  
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5 in context of abnormal ECG, or lactate >4 mmol/L, or GCS <13. For STEMI, do not wait for scores – escalate based on ECG findings.

rawMarkdown:
## ECG — Performing
**One-liner:** Recording a diagnostic 12-lead ECG; essential for detecting arrhythmia, ischaemia, and conduction disease; poor technique causes misdiagnosis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **STEMI:** Patient with chest pain, new left bundle branch block (LBBB) or ST elevation ≥1mm in limb leads / ≥2mm in chest leads. This is the waveform that mandates immediate senior review, aspirin, and catheter lab activation. Do not finish the ECG if you see evolving ST elevation – show the tracing to a senior immediately.
- **Malignant arrhythmia on the tracing:** While performing, the screen shows ventricular tachycardia, torsades de pointes, or extreme bradycardia (<30 bpm). If the patient is symptomatic (syncope, hypotension), call for help — don’t remove leads; start CPR if no pulse.

### Presentation
- **Indications for performing (typical scenarios):**  
  - Chest pain, dyspnoea, palpitations, syncope, pre-operative assessment, altered mental status, trauma (especially high-velocity).
- **Key exam findings affecting technique:**  
  - Patient’s chest shape (e.g., pectus excavatum, breast tissue, obesity) → may require alternative electrode positions.  
  - Hairy chest → requires shaving for electrode contact.  
  - Tremor (e.g., Parkinson’s, anxiety) → may cause muscle artifact – reposition arms, ask patient to relax.  
  - Inability to lie flat (e.g., severe COPD, heart failure) → record semi-upright; note on tracing.

### Differential Diagnosis
(Not a differential diagnosis list – instead, differentiate between true abnormality vs. artifact during acquisition; this primes pattern recognition on the tracing.)
- **True ST elevation** (MI, pericarditis, early repolarisation) vs. **artifact** (e.g., muscle tremor, wandering baseline due to poor skin contact, lead swap – e.g., misplaced V3 giving a false Q wave).
- **Sinus arrhythmia** (normal) vs. **atrial fibrillation** (irregularly irregular) – check lead II rhythm strip length and patient symptoms.
- **LBBB** vs. **paced rhythm** – look for pacer spikes before each QRS; if no spikes, it’s LBBB.

### Investigations
The ECG itself *is* the investigation. This section covers how to perform it correctly.

- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Confirm patient identity, indication, and that they have given verbal consent (if conscious).  
  - Position patient supine (or semi-recumbent if unable). Arms relaxed, legs uncrossed.  
  - Clean skin – alcohol wipe or dry gauze; shave hair if necessary. Remove any oily film or moisturiser.  
  - Apply limb electrodes to fleshy parts of forearms and calves – **not** over bone or major tendon.  
  - Apply precordial electrodes:  
    - V1: 4th intercostal space (ICS), right sternal edge.  
    - V2: 4th ICS, left sternal edge.  
    - V3: midway between V2 and V4.  
    - V4: 5th ICS, midclavicular line.  
    - V5: 5th ICS, anterior axillary line (or horizontally from V4).  
    - V6: 5th ICS, midaxillary line.  
  - **Crucial:** In women, place V4–V6 *under* the breast tissue (by lifting the breast), not on top of it.  
  - Ensure all electrodes are firmly attached – poor contact causes wandering baseline.  
  - Press the “Auto” record button – machine will record 10 seconds of each lead.  
  - Observe the screen during recording:  
    - If artifact appears (e.g., baseline wander, 50 Hz interference), check leads and ask patient to breathe normally or hold breath briefly.  
    - If muscle tremor, ensure patient’s arms and legs are relaxed – may need to place arm electrodes higher on shoulders.  
- **Machine settings (verify before pressing record):**  
  - Paper speed 25 mm/sec (standard).  
  - Calibration 10 mm/mV (standard).  
  - Filter settings: use “diagnostic” mode (0.05-100 Hz) – avoid “monitor” filter (0.5-40 Hz) which can distort ST segment.  
- **When to repeat or add leads:**  
  - If patient has inferior ST elevation (II, III, aVF) → record right-sided ECG (V4R placed at 5th ICS right midclavicular line) to detect right ventricular involvement.  
  - If patient has posterior suspicion (e.g., anterior ST depression, tall R in V1) → record posterior leads V7 (left posterior axillary line at 5th ICS) and V8 (left mid-scapular line).  
- **Bloods (if ECG abnormal):** troponin, U&E (for hyperkalaemia), glucose (for hypokalaemia) – but these are not part of the performance itself.  
- **Imaging:** Not required as part of ECG performance.  
- **Special tests:** For suspected arrhythmias intermittent, consider ambulatory monitoring (Holter) or event loop recorder – not relevant at point-of-care 12-lead.  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** The **Modified Sgarbossa criteria** for diagnosing STEMI in the presence of LBBB:  
  - Concordant ST elevation ≥1mm in leads with positive QRS.  
  - Concordant ST depression ≥1mm in V1–V3.  
  - Discordant ST elevation ≥5mm.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Right-sided ECG** in any patient with inferior ST elevation – omission can miss right ventricular infarction, which changes fluid management (avoid nitrates, fill with IV fluids).  

### Management
(Management of the procedure, not of the underlying condition.)

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Perform ECG as above.  
  - If patient is unstable (chest pain, hypotension, shock), prioritise treatment over perfect technique – a 4-lead rhythm strip may be faster; however, a 12-lead should follow ASAP.  
  - If you observe ST elevation or new LBBB, show the tracing to a senior doctor immediately (do not wait for machine interpretation).  
  - Label the ECG clearly: patient name, date, time, indication.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - For patients with abnormal ECG, ensure the tracing is reviewed by a clinician within 15 minutes (locally defined).  
  - Repeat ECG for any new symptoms or after interventions (e.g., thrombolysis, PCI).  
  - If poor quality (artifact, missing leads), re-record – do not accept suboptimal traces.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Not applicable to performing the initial ECG.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - No specific orders for the procedure itself. If ECG shows ACS, follow local chest pain pathway (e.g., aspirin 300mg PO, GTN 400mcg SL, monitoring).  

### Drugs & Doses
No drugs are required for performing an ECG. However, if during the procedure you identify STEMI or tachyarrhythmia needing immediate treatment:  
- **First-line agents for suspected ACS (per NICE):**  
  - Aspirin 300 mg orally, stat (no contraindication).  
  - Glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) 400–800 mcg sublingually, repeated if needed (avoid if systolic BP <90 mmHg or suspected right ventricular infarction).  
- **For symptomatic bradycardia:** Atropine 500 mcg IV bolus, repeated up to 3 mg (per UK Resuscitation Council).  
- **Key contraindications:** GTN is contraindicated in patients who have taken sildenafil/vardenafil in past 24 hours (or tadalafil in past 48 hours). Atropine – avoid in haemodynamically unstable non-sinus rhythms (e.g., third-degree AV block – use transcutaneous pacing instead).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for acute doses.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving GTN sublingually before checking for right ventricular infarction (inferior STEMI). This can cause profound hypotension. Always consider right-sided ECG before giving GTN.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Patient vasovagal syncope (due to anxiety, lying flat, or pain from electrode removal) – remove electrodes, lie supine, raise legs.  
  - Skin irritation or allergic reaction to electrode gel – use hypoallergenic electrodes if known allergy.  
  - Missed ischaemia due to artifact – re-record if any doubt about quality.  
- **Chronic:**  
  - None from the procedure itself.  

### Prognosis
The ECG is a diagnostic tool – prognosis depends on the underlying condition. A normal ECG in a chest pain patient does not rule out MI (especially NSTEMI); proceed with troponin.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Shave hairy chests before applying electrodes** – it reduces artifact and improves ST-segment fidelity. Use a disposable razor; avoid alcohol wipes on abraded skin.  
- **Document any lead placement deviation** directly on the ECG (e.g., "V4 placed under right breast," "patient sitting up due to dyspnoea") – this prevents false interpretations.  
- **Perform a right-sided ECG in any inferior STEMI** – it changes management (avoid nitrates, give fluids). Don't rely on standard 12-lead.  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Placing limb electrodes on bony prominences or over major tendons** – causes muscle tremor artifact. Use fleshy parts of forearms and calves.  
- **Placing V4-V6 on top of breast tissue in women** – leads to small QRS voltages and falsely poor R-wave progression. Lift the breast to place electrodes in correct intercostal spaces.  
- **Accepting a poor-quality tracing** – baseline wander, 50 Hz interference, or missing leads can mask ST elevation or arrhythmias. Always re-record if the trace is not clean.  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New chest pain, palpitations, or syncope.  
  - ECG result discussed with patient? If abnormal but deemed non-urgent, explain findings simply and mention that symptoms require re-attendance.  
  - If ECG was normal but patient had symptoms, instruct to re-present if symptoms recur.  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - If abnormal (e.g., left ventricular hypertrophy, old MI), arrange GP follow-up or cardiology outpatient, depending on local policy.  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**  
  - ECG shows ST elevation, new LBBB, or clear signs of acute MI.  
  - Rhythm strip shows VT, torsades, asystole, or extreme bradycardia with symptoms.  
- **Call crash team if:** patient becomes unconscious, pulseless, or has a respiratory arrest while leads are attached.  
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5 in context of abnormal ECG, or lactate >4 mmol/L, or GCS <13. For STEMI, do not wait for scores – escalate based on ECG findings.
